---
title: "Dyad-Day"
subtitle: "Dyadic Data Analysis with R and interdep"
author:
  - "Turu"
  - "Pascal"
bibliography: ../../vignettes/references.bib
format:
  revealjs:
    css: workshop.css
    logo: uzh-logo.svg
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    preview-links: auto
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
editor: source
---

<!--

# Planning advice

Use a hybrid production sequence:

1. Build the workshop skeleton immediately.
2. Finish and stabilize only the cross-sectional APIM vignette.
3. Turn the stable APIM and DIM content into workshop-specific materials.
4. Return to broader vignette polishing after the workshop.

Do not finish the entire APIM vignette first, but do not develop the statistical
content independently inside the slides either. The vignette should remain the
technical source of truth; the workshop should be a deliberately simplified
teaching layer.

The APIM vignette already contains the full cross-sectional sequence:
distinguishable, exchangeable, residual structure, interpretation, and testing
distinguishability. It mainly needs consolidation and teaching polish. The DIM
cross-sectional material is already extensive enough to extract from.

## Most important conceptual advice

The overall progression is good:

APIM → exchangeable APIM → DIM → DSM → CFM.

Avoid describing this too literally as a progression from the “most
individual” to the “most dyadic” model. These are different representations
and assumptions, not a strict continuum of dyadicness.

A useful recurring question is:

> What aspect of the dyad does this model treat as the explanatory unit?

- APIM: each member’s predictor, separated into actor and partner contributions.
- DIM: the dyad’s shared predictor level and each member’s deviation.
- DSM: predictor level and directed difference predicting outcome level and
  directed difference.
- CFM: a shared latent construct measured imperfectly by members.

Emphasize that APIM, DIM, and DSM can sometimes be algebraic
reparameterizations or constrained versions of one another. They are not
always competing explanations that produce different fitted values.

## Suggested adjustment to Session 1

The listed model allocations total 50 rather than 45 minutes, and the complete
first session leaves effectively no transition buffer.

Suggested allocation:

- Introductions and aims: 20 minutes
- Why dyadic data are special: 5 minutes
- Model landscape: 40 minutes
- Comparison and decision table: 10 minutes
- Participant activity and discussion: 25 minutes
- Buffer: 5 minutes

Possible model-landscape allocation:

- Distinguishable APIM: 8 minutes
- Exchangeable APIM: 7 minutes
- DIM: 8 minutes
- DSM: 8 minutes
- CFM: 7 minutes
- Transitions: 2 minutes

The comparison table should probably appear in the main presentation rather
than only in the appendix. Useful columns are:

| Model | Basic unit | Role assumption | Predictor representation | Outcome representation | Best-suited question |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|

Omit Truth and Bias from the taught sequence. A final “other dyadic models”
resources slide is sufficient.

## Testing distinguishability

Teach this as testing whether role-specific parameters can reasonably be
constrained to equality.

Distinguishability is partly a substantive and design property. A
nonsignificant comparison does not prove that members are inherently
indistinguishable. It supports pooling the particular parameters under the
tested constraints.

## Detailed tutorial

Session 2 currently fills all 90 minutes exactly. Live code, questions, and
minor technical problems will consume time.

Suggested allocation:

- Framework and software overview: 10–15 minutes
- Distinguishable APIM: 20 minutes
- Exchangeable APIM: 15 minutes
- Equality constraints and model comparison: 10 minutes
- DIM and APIM–DIM equivalence: 15 minutes
- Short interpretation task: 10 minutes
- Buffer: 5–10 minutes

Use one shared example and repeatedly ask participants to interpret one
highlighted coefficient. This is likely more useful than displaying several
complete model summaries.

## Intensive longitudinal section

Clearly distinguish:

- Concurrent ILD APIM: predictors and outcomes at the same occasion.
- Lagged or dynamic APIM: earlier states predicting later outcomes.
- Residual serial dependence: remaining temporal correlation, which is not
  itself a substantive dynamic effect.

For this workshop, fully demonstrate one concurrent ILD APIM and present
dynamic models as a guided preview using prepared output. Avoid relying on a
complex dynamic model fitting successfully during live teaching.

## Exercises

Define three core exercises:

1. Fit and interpret a distinguishable APIM.
2. Fit the constrained exchangeable APIM and compare it with the
   distinguishable model.
3. Transform the APIM coefficients into DIM coefficients and interpret the
   result.

Provide these as extensions:

- Fit the DIM directly and verify equivalence.
- Fit an ILD APIM.
- Explore the DSM vignette.
- Try a generalized model.

Provide partially completed scripts, explicit questions, and solution files.
Late in the day, participants will learn more from interpretation and
modification than from typing model formulas from scratch.

## Two-week production sequence

1. Create the slide and exercise skeleton, including fixed learning objectives
   and timing placeholders.
2. Finish the cross-sectional APIM vignette to workshop-ready quality.
3. Extract figures, model code, fitted values, and explanations from APIM and
   DIM.
4. Write workshop-specific slides and exercises rather than shortened copies
   of the vignettes.
5. Create one tested “golden script” containing every model used during the
   workshop.
6. Conduct a full timed rehearsal, freeze code and outputs, and keep only bug
   fixes afterward.

The materials should have distinct functions:

- Slides explain the conceptual story.
- A handout contains concise formulas and interpretation guidance.
- Scripts contain runnable code.
- Vignettes remain the detailed reference.
- Solutions contain the complete analysis and expected output.

Reserve at least one full day for a timed rehearsal.

# Original program

## Dyad-Day

### 11:00–12:45: Session 1 (105 Minutes)

- Introducing Turu & Pascal (5 Minutes?)
- Introductions of participants (name, field, dyadic experience, one sentence
  on data-type of current project/problem) (10 Minutes)
- Workshop Aims & Program (5 Minutes)

#### Part 1: Introducing different dyadic models (conceptually only) (80 Minutes)

- What is dyadic data (analysis) and why is it challenging? (5 Minutes)
- Overview over different types of dyadic models (45 Minutes):

  Dyadic data can be modeled in many different ways, depending on the data and
  research question! Here are some interesting approaches, from the most
  “individual” to the most “dyadic” model. Conceptual only, with code only in
  the appendix. We can provide output with numeric examples using the same
  variables for each model to demonstrate interpretation:

  - Distinguishable APIM (most common) (max. 10 Minutes; short since it will be
    a focus later on)
    - Individual → individual (interdependent)
  - Indistinguishable/exchangeable APIM (10 Minutes)
    - Individual → individual (interdependent)
  - Dyad-Individual Model; DIM (10 Minutes)
    - Dyad-level/deviation → individual
    - Or can be interpreted as dyad-level → dyad-level; deviation → deviation,
      but without cross-paths
  - Dyadic Score Model; DSM (10 Minutes)
    - Dyad-level/directed deviation → dyad-level/directed deviation
    - Adds cross-paths compared to the DIM
  - Common Fate Model (10 Minutes)
    - Latent dyadic construct → latent dyadic construct (individual differences
      are residual)
    - This is last because it is somewhat similar to the dyad level of the DSM,
      but also very different in what a latent construct means versus a simple
      mean.
  - Truth and Bias model (optional brief mention, since it is conceptually
    different. Probably omit to avoid overwhelming participants.) Perhaps have
    a slide that is skipped but points participants to resources.

- Brief recap/summary of when to choose which model! (Table in appendix)
  (5 Minutes)
- Interactive session (considering 7–10 people) (30 Minutes, can be shortened
  and used as buffer):
  - Brief Q&A (10 Minutes)
  - Optional brief exercise in which participants consider their own potential
    research questions and which model or models would be appropriate and why.
    Participants without their own dyadic research question receive 3–4
    questions to consider. (10 Minutes)
  - Group discussion (10 Minutes)

### 12:45–14:00: Lunch Break (75 Minutes)

### 14:00–15:30: Session 2 (90 Minutes)

#### Part 2 (short): Which framework and software to use when? (max. 15 Minutes)

- MLM (Multilevel) or SEM? (5–10 Minutes; a brief summary of the main points,
  and note that this is provided in the slides to help participants make their
  decision.)

  In many simpler cases, the two frameworks estimate equivalent models and the
  choice is a matter of preference. In more complex scenarios, there are
  additional considerations.

  You may consider favoring SEM when:

  - Latent modelling might be beneficial, such as with certain types of missing
    data, measurement models, Dynamic SEM, lagged effects, or mediation.

  You may consider favoring MLM when:

  - There are complex nested or crossed grouping structures; a need for more
    robust and varied support for non-Gaussian distributions, such as
    zero-inflated or hurdle models; complex models requiring flexible
    interactions, splines, or nonlinear effects; or a preference or need for a
    more mature workflow for predictive checks and distributional diagnostics.

  In this workshop, we will probably mostly show the MLM approach. It is still
  recommended to learn both frameworks eventually and choose the best one for
  the data and research questions.

  Optional brief note: It can be worthwhile to learn Bayesian modelling and use
  Bayesian SEM and Bayesian MLM, for example with brms, because priors can help
  stabilize models alongside benefits in reporting and interpretation.

- Why are we using R instead of SPSS, SAS, or Mplus? (5 Minutes max.)

  R is open, free, reproducible, scriptable, and offers packages that are very
  flexible for observed-variable MLMs and GLMMs, including non-Gaussian cases.
  SPSS and SAS are more mature specifically for Gaussian mixed models with
  residual covariance structures over time, such as Kronecker AR1@UN. Mplus is
  still particularly strong for complex SEM and DSEM. lavaan, blavaan, or
  dynamite in R can be viable options for some simpler SEM models.

#### Part 3: Detailed tutorial and walkthrough of selected cross-sectional Gaussian models (75 Minutes)

Estimating these models with glmmTMB and discussing assumptions:

- Cross-sectional APIM for distinguishable dyads (20 Minutes)
- Cross-sectional APIM for indistinguishable dyads (20 Minutes)
- Testing distinguishability with model comparison and Wald test (10 Minutes)
- Cross-sectional DIM for indistinguishable dyads and demonstrating the
  equivalence (20 Minutes)
- Mention very briefly that generalized non-Gaussian models are possible but,
  depending on the family, require additional assumptions and constraints due
  to additional parameters and non-identifiability issues. Generalized models
  are often a much easier extension in ILD. Brief example if there is enough
  time (buffer).
- Q&A / Buffer (5 Minutes)

### 15:30–15:45: Short Break (15 Minutes)

### 15:45–16:45: Session 3 (60 Minutes)

#### Part 4: Extending to the intensive longitudinal case (60 Minutes)

Extending to the intensive longitudinal case as a brief applied tutorial
without too much theory, since participants already know ILD analysis and this
could fill its own full day:

- AR1 versus dynamic models and current limitations and developments in R
  (15 Minutes)
- Distinguishable dynamic APIM (15 Minutes)
- Indistinguishable dynamic APIM (15 Minutes)
- Dynamic DIM (5 Minutes preview only, demonstrating equivalence on both levels
  and referring to online materials)

#### Part 4.5: Extension to generalized MLMs (buffer only; approximately 15 Minutes)

Very briefly mention or show one or two examples of a generalized non-Gaussian
APIM or DIM with a negative binomial outcome and point to the example code.

### 16:45–17:00: Short Break (15 Minutes)

### 17:00–18:00: Part 5: Exercises (60 Minutes)

- Cross-sectional Gaussian APIM, distinguishable
- Cross-sectional Gaussian APIM, indistinguishable
- Test distinguishability
- ILD APIM, distinguishable
- ILD APIM, indistinguishable
- Compute and interpret DIM estimates directly from APIM models
- Optional: Estimate the DIM and compare it with the manually computed DIM
  estimates
- For participants who finish very quickly, refer to the DSM documentation
  online; they can try it with the example data.

-->

<!-- Workshop slide content starts here. -->

## Today

| Time | Focus |
|---|---|
| 11:00–12:45 | Introduction and conceptual model landscape |
| 12:45–14:00 | Lunch break |
| 14:00–15:30 | Frameworks and cross-sectional models |
| 15:30–15:45 | Short break |
| 15:45–16:45 | Intensive longitudinal extensions |
| 16:45–17:00 | Short break |
| 17:00–18:00 | Exercises in R |

# Session 1: Conceptual foundations

## Welcome and introductions

## Workshop aims and program

# Part 1: Introducing dyadic models

## What is dyadic data analysis—and why is it challenging?

## A landscape of dyadic models

## Distinguishable APIM

## Exchangeable APIM

## Dyad–Individual Model (DIM)

## Dyadic Score Model (DSM)

## Common Fate Model (CFM)

## When should we choose which model?

## Apply the models to your own research questions

## Questions and group discussion

# Lunch break

# Session 2: Cross-sectional models

# Part 2: Choosing a framework and software

## Multilevel modelling or SEM?

## Why are we using R?

# Part 3: Cross-sectional Gaussian models

## Distinguishable APIM

## Exchangeable APIM

## Testing distinguishability

## Exchangeable DIM and APIM–DIM equivalence

## Generalized non-Gaussian models

## Questions and buffer

# Short break

# Session 3: Intensive longitudinal models

# Part 4: Extending to intensive longitudinal data

## AR(1) versus dynamic models

## Distinguishable dynamic APIM

## Exchangeable dynamic APIM

## Dynamic DIM

# Part 4.5: Generalized multilevel models

# Short break

# Part 5: Exercises in R

## Cross-sectional distinguishable APIM

## Cross-sectional exchangeable APIM

## Testing distinguishability

## Intensive longitudinal distinguishable APIM

## Intensive longitudinal exchangeable APIM

## Computing and interpreting DIM estimates from an APIM

## Estimating the DIM directly

## Further exploration: DSM
